section.title: The Useless Vine
section.sectionRationale: Israel had long pictured herself as God's chosen vine, but Ezekiel strips away every illusion of inherent value in the vine's own wood. Unlike sturdy timber, vine wood is useless for anything but bearing fruit; charred by fire and fruitless, Jerusalem has become good for nothing at all except further burning, a brief, brutal parable that clears the ground for the fuller vine and bride imagery to come.
section.thesis: The wood of a vine has no worth in itself, no strength for building, no use as a peg, its entire value lies in bearing fruit; so Jerusalem, having borne no fruit of faithfulness and having already been scorched once by judgment, is fit only to be consumed further, a warning that finds its full resolution only in the true, fruitful vine, Jesus Christ, and the branches that abide in Him.
section.frameNote: Two movements: the vine's inherent uselessness apart from fruit, even when whole, unable to serve as timber or a simple peg; and the direct application of that uselessness to Jerusalem, already touched by one fire and now given over to another.

units.label: Wood good for nothing
units.note: the vine's uselessness apart from fruit

verses.text: Then the word of the LORD came to me, saying,
“Son of man, how does the wood of the {{gephen|vine}} surpass any other branch among the trees in the forest?
Can wood be taken from it to make something useful? Or can one make from it a peg on which to hang utensils?
No, it is cast into the fire for fuel. The fire devours both ends, and the middle is charred. Can it be useful for anything?
Even when it was whole, it could not be made useful. How much less can it ever be useful when the fire has consumed it and charred it!
Therefore this is what the Lord GOD says: ‘Like the wood of the vine among the trees of the forest, which I have given to the fire for fuel, so I will give up the people of Jerusalem.
And I will set My face against them. Though they may have escaped the fire, yet another fire will consume them. And when I set My face against them, you will know that I am the LORD.
Thus I will make the land desolate, because they have acted {{maal|unfaithfully}},’ declares the Lord GOD.”

gephen.gloss: Vine. Israel is repeatedly pictured as God's vine or vineyard, Psalm 80:8-16, Isaiah 5:1-7, Hosea 10:1; here the image is stripped of all sentiment, focusing solely on the wood's uselessness apart from bearing fruit.

maal.gloss: Treachery, unfaithfulness, a breach of trust, often used of covenant betrayal. The land's desolation is directly tied to this ma'al, the same term used elsewhere in Scripture for Israel's persistent covenant infidelity.

basic.context: Israel loved to picture itself as God's vine, a symbol of privilege and promise. Ezekiel dismantles that comfort with a blunt question: apart from fruit, what is vine wood actually good for? Not even a peg. Having already been scorched once by judgment and still bearing no fruit, Jerusalem is fit only for further burning.
basic.hermeneutics: This is a short, sharp parable applied directly and explicitly to Jerusalem by God Himself in verse 6, not left open to interpretation. Its plain, uncomfortable point is that privilege without fruit carries no independent value; being the vine means everything if it bears fruit, and nothing at all if it does not.
basic.originalLanguages: The gephen, vine, was Israel's favorite self-image across the Psalms and Prophets, but here its wood is shown structurally worthless apart from fruit-bearing, unlike timber trees prized for their wood itself. The land's coming desolation is tied to Israel's ma'al, unfaithfulness, a term elsewhere describing serious covenant betrayal, not a minor lapse.
basic.typology: The useless, fruitless vine cast into the fire anticipates Jesus' own words in John 15:1-6, where He identifies Himself as 'the true vine' and warns that any branch not abiding in Him and bearing fruit 'is thrown away and withers... thrown into the fire and burned', a direct echo and fulfillment of this exact image.
basic.christ: Where Jerusalem, the failed vine, could only be judged and burned for its fruitlessness, Jesus presents Himself as the true and faithful vine, John 15:1, in whom His people, as branches, can finally bear the fruit Israel could not produce on her own; apart from Him people remain like this useless wood, but abiding in Him, much fruit is genuinely possible.
basic.god: God is unsentimental about privilege detached from fruitfulness; being chosen as His vine carries real responsibility, not automatic protection, and He will not spare a fruitless people simply because of their favored status or history.
basic.crossReferences: Psalm 80:8-16 pictures Israel as a vine brought out of Egypt. Isaiah 5:1-7 tells the song of the vineyard that yielded only wild grapes. Hosea 10:1 calls Israel a luxuriant vine. John 15:1-6 has Jesus declare Himself the true vine whose fruitless branches are burned.
basic.oneStory: This is the collapse of Israel's favorite self-image, a vine whose wood, apart from fruit, is worth nothing at all. The story does not end with fruitless wood destined for the fire; it runs forward to Jesus, the true vine, who makes genuine fruitfulness possible for every branch that abides in Him.

context.literary: This brief, tightly constructed parable functions as a hinge between the earlier oracles of judgment and the extended, deliberately shocking allegories of chapters 16 and 23, using the same vine imagery that reappears explicitly in chapter 17's eagles and vine and implicitly behind chapter 19's lament.
context.historical: Delivered to exiles who likely still clung to Israel's status as God's chosen vine as a guarantee of future protection, this parable directly confronts that assumption before Jerusalem's final destruction in 586 BC.
context.canonical: Draws on the long-standing vine and vineyard imagery of Psalm 80 and Isaiah 5, and anticipates its fullest New Testament development in John 15, where Jesus reclaims and fulfills the image as the true vine.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To strip away Israel's comfortable self-image as God's protected vine by showing that vine wood, apart from fruit, has no worth at all, and to apply that stark truth directly to Jerusalem's coming, deserved destruction.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This is a specific, applied parable pronouncing judgment on Jerusalem; it is not a general prescription about literal vines or wood use. The enduring truth is that any people, community, or individual who bears the name of God's people without bearing corresponding fruit has no independent claim to value or protection.

debates.question: Does the phrase 'though they may have escaped the fire, yet another fire will consume them' in verse 7 refer to a previous historical judgment already experienced by Jerusalem?

views.label: A reference to earlier partial judgments
views.case: Some see this as pointing to earlier deportations or disasters, such as the 597 BC exile of Jehoiachin and other leaders, which Jerusalem survived, only to face a still greater fire in the coming final destruction of 586 BC.

views.label: A rhetorical intensification within the parable itself
views.case: Others read it primarily as continuing the wood imagery, wood charred at both ends by a first fire is even less useful when a second fire comes, intensifying the parable's logic rather than making a specific historical reference.

debates.bearing: Either reading reinforces the same point: whatever partial judgments Jerusalem has already survived offer no security against the fuller judgment still to come if fruitfulness and faithfulness do not follow.

study.originalLanguages: The gephen, vine, was Israel's cherished self-image throughout the Psalms and Prophets, yet its wood is shown here to be structurally worthless apart from bearing fruit, unlike valuable timber trees. The coming desolation of the land is tied directly to Israel's ma'al, unfaithfulness, a term used elsewhere for serious covenant betrayal rather than minor failure.

typology.type: The fruitless vine wood fit only for the fire, Ezekiel 15:1-6
typology.fulfillment: Directly anticipated and echoed in John 15:1-6, where Jesus warns that branches not abiding in Him, the true vine, are thrown into the fire and burned.

study.christ: Where Jerusalem, the failed vine, could only be judged and burned for her fruitlessness, Jesus presents Himself as the true and faithful vine, John 15:1, in whom His people, as branches, can finally bear the fruit Israel could not produce by her own privilege alone. Apart from Him people remain like this useless charred wood, but abiding in Him, genuine and lasting fruit becomes possible.
study.god: God is unsentimental about privilege detached from fruitfulness; being chosen as His vine carries real responsibility, not automatic protection, and He will not spare a fruitless people simply because of their favored status, history, or past deliverances.

crossReferences.note: Pictures Israel as a vine God brought out of Egypt and planted.
The song of the vineyard that, despite every care, yielded only wild grapes.
Calls Israel a luxuriant vine that produced fruit only for herself, not for God.
Jesus declares Himself the true vine whose fruitless branches are gathered and burned.

study.oneStory: This is the collapse of Israel's favorite self-image, a vine whose wood, apart from fruit, is worth nothing at all. The story does not end with fruitless wood destined for the fire; it runs forward to Jesus, the true vine, who makes genuine and lasting fruitfulness possible for every branch that abides in Him.

quiz.q: According to Ezekiel 15:2-3, how does the wood of a vine compare to timber from a forest tree?

options.text: It has no comparable use; it cannot even be made into a simple peg.
options.note: Verses 2-3 make this exact point through rhetorical questions expecting a negative answer.

options.text: It is stronger and more valuable than any other wood.
options.note: The passage argues the opposite: vine wood has no structural value at all.

options.text: It is equally useful for building houses.
options.note: The text explicitly denies the vine wood any building use.

options.text: It is prized mainly for its beautiful grain.
options.note: No aesthetic value is mentioned; the point is its lack of practical usefulness.

quiz.q: What is the vine's wood good for, according to this passage?

options.text: Only for fuel, being cast into the fire.
options.note: Verse 4 states the wood is cast into the fire for fuel, its only remaining use once it is not bearing fruit.

options.text: For making fine furniture.
options.note: The passage denies any refined or practical use for the wood itself.

options.text: For crafting tools and pegs.
options.note: Verse 3 explicitly denies this exact use.

options.text: For decorative garden trellises.
options.note: No such use is mentioned; the text focuses narrowly on fuel as the wood's only remaining purpose.

quiz.q: To whom does God directly apply the image of the useless, charred vine wood?

options.text: The people of Jerusalem.
options.note: Verse 6 states plainly, 'so I will give up the people of Jerusalem.'

options.text: The nations surrounding Israel.
options.note: The application is specifically and directly to Jerusalem, not the surrounding nations.

options.text: Only the false prophets described in the previous chapters.
options.note: The application is to the people of Jerusalem generally, not narrowly to the false prophets.

options.text: The exiles already living in Babylon.
options.note: The judgment described is specifically against Jerusalem and its inhabitants, not the exiled community receiving the message.

quiz.q: What does verse 7 mean by saying, 'though they may have escaped the fire, yet another fire will consume them'?

options.text: Even those who survived an earlier judgment will face a further, complete judgment.
options.note: This reflects the wood's charred condition from an earlier fire, now facing complete consumption by a second, decisive fire.

options.text: God promises complete protection from any future disaster.
options.note: The verse warns of further judgment, not protection from it.

options.text: The fire refers only to a literal accidental fire in Jerusalem's history.
options.note: The fire is figurative, representing God's ongoing judgment, not a literal historical accident.

options.text: It promises that no further judgment will ever come upon the people.
options.note: The verse promises the opposite, a further and more complete judgment still to come.

quiz.q: What underlying reason does God give in verse 8 for making the land desolate?

options.text: Because the people acted unfaithfully, a direct covenant betrayal.
options.note: Verse 8 explicitly names 'they have acted unfaithfully' as the reason for the coming desolation.

options.text: Because the land itself was naturally unproductive.
options.note: The desolation is judgment for unfaithfulness, not a natural agricultural deficiency.

options.text: Because foreign armies simply happened to invade.
options.note: The desolation is presented as God's direct judgment for unfaithfulness, not incidental to foreign invasion alone.

options.text: Because the people had grown too numerous for the land to support.
options.note: Overpopulation is not the stated reason; covenant unfaithfulness is.

questions: Israel took comfort in being God's vine without bearing fruit to match that identity. What privileges or labels, being part of a church, a Christian family, or a long history of faith, might you be leaning on instead of actually bearing fruit?
The vine's wood has no independent value apart from fruit. Where in your life have you confused having a good reputation or position with actually producing the fruit of a transformed life?

Jerusalem had already been scorched by one judgment yet still bore no fruit, inviting a second, greater fire. What warnings or hardships in your own life might God be using to call you toward repentance before something more serious follows?

Jesus reclaims this exact image, calling Himself the true vine whose branches bear real fruit only by abiding in Him. What practical habits help you stay abiding in Christ this week rather than trying to bear fruit through your own effort?

This parable is unsentimental about privilege without fruitfulness. Where might you need to let go of assuming God's favor is automatic, and instead ask honestly whether your life is bearing the fruit that identity implies?

translationNotes.note: The rhetorical question 'how does the wood of the vine surpass any other branch' expects the answer 'it does not', setting up the parable's entire argument that vine wood has no independent structural value.

The description of the fire devouring 'both ends' while the 'middle is charred' pictures a piece of vine wood already partially burned as kindling, useless even before any further fire comes, intensifying the image of total worthlessness.

The term rendered 'acted unfaithfully' carries the sense of a serious breach of trust or covenant betrayal, the same root used throughout the Old Testament for grave unfaithfulness to God, not a minor or accidental lapse.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched via the Makor scripture toolkit. No sermon from the trusted cluster (Begg, Keller, Piper, Ferguson, Carson) specifically and primarily addressing Ezekiel 15 was found this session, only a brief passing reference within a sermon on Ezekiel 16 and 23, so sermon status is none rather than a loosely connected citation.
